FADE IN: Scene EXT. SUNNY SKY - DAY We start high in the clouds and slowly DROP DOWN to the 0ol' Springfield fishin' hole. There's a sign saying "Ol' Fishin' Hole," beneath this is a small "tm". The mood is very bucolic and idyllic. Two barefooted children, BART SIMPSON and his younger sister LISA, are fishing with makeshift tree-branch fishing poles. Bart is chewing on a stalk of wheat. LISA You know, Bart, there's so few times in life that you get to feel like a real kid -- BART (UNDER HIS BREATH) Shut up. LISA -- but right now, sitting here at the ol' fishin' hole -- BART

(UNDER HIS BREATH) Shut up.

REVISED TABLE DRAFT 3/20/90 PAGE 2.

Scene 1 (con)

LISA -- with the mud oozing through my wriggling, young toes -- BART (UNDER HIS BREATH) Shut up.

During the following, Bart leans in until he is glaring at Lisa, nose to nose.

LISA -- I can't help but think --

BART

Shut up! LISA

-- that this is the best BART

Shut up! LISA

-- of all possible worlds -- BART

SHUT UP!!!

Seene 2

They go back to fishing. A car PULLS UP on the hill behind Bart and Lisa. A Spencer Tracy type man, DAVE SHUTTON, wearing a suit and snapbrim hat, gets out of the car to stretch his legs. With his tie askew, his jacket

folded over one arm and a big smile, he trots down to Bart and Lisa.

DAVE SHUTTON So kids, caught anything? LISA

Not yet, sir.

REVISED TABLE DRAFT 3/20/90 PAGE 3.

Scene Z (con't)

DAVE SHUTTON What are you using for bait? LISA My brother's using worms but I who feel that the tranquility far outweighs the actual catching of fish, am using nothing. DAVE SHUTTON I see. And what's your name, son? BART I'm Bart Simpson. Who the hell are you? DAVE SHUTTON I'm Dave Shutton. I'm an investigative reporter who's on the road a lot, and I must say that in my day we didn't talk that way to our elders. BART That was then, this is now, sir. DAVE SHUTTON Yeah, things change, don't they? Except at the ol' fishin' hole. Mind if I take off my shoes and join you? BART Not if you shut up.

There's a jerk on Bart's fishing pole.

REVISED TABLE DRAFT 3/20/90 PAGE 4. Scene 2 (con't) BART All right! We eat tonight! Bart YANKS on the pole and pulls in a large fish. CLOSE-UP ON FISH We see that it has three eyes, which blink in succession. BACK TO WIDE SHOT DAVE SHUTTON Wait a minute. (STARTS COUNTING) One, two, three. The fish blinks its three eyes as they are counted. CLOSE-UP ON DAVE SHUTTON A concerned and resolute look comes over his face. A PAN past his look reveals that the ol' fishin' hole is in the shadow of the power plant. A drainage pipe is spewing radioactive by-products into the fishing hole. We see a MONTAGE of spinning newspaper headlines: A. "MUTATION CAUGHT AT OL' FISHIN' HOLE." There's a picture of Bart posing proudly holding the fish upside down. The sub-headline reads: "IS POWER PLANT RESPONSIBLE?" The sub sub headline reads "BOY WAS USING FIVE POUND TEST AND ORDINARY WORMS". Sub sub sub headline, "SISTER WAS JUST THERE FOR THE TRANQUILITY." B. "FISHIN' HOLE OR FISSION HOLE?" Sub headline: "BURNS DENIES RESPONSIBILITY IN FISH FLAP". The Sub-sub headline reads: "COUNT THE EYES MR. BURNS!", there's a posed publicity photo of Burns, however, he is scowling. C. "GOVERNOR CALLS FOR POWER PLANT INVESTIGATION." We see a picture of state governor MARY BAILEY, a sweet-looking woman in her dotage. INT. SIMPSON KITCHEN - MORNING Seene 3 MARGE (V.0.) Well leave it to good old Mary Bailey

to finally step in and do something

about that hideous genetic mutation.

REVISED TABLE DRAFT 3/20/90 PAGE 5.

Scene 3 (con's)

We PULL BACK revealing the family seated around the kitchen table having breakfast. MARGE is reading the newspaper.

HOMER (SNORTS) Mary Bailey. Well, if I was governor, I'd sure find better things to do with my time. MARGE Like what? HOMER Like getting Washington's birthday and Lincoln's birthday back as separate paid holidays. (DERISIVELY) President's Day! What a rip-off! I bust my butt day in and day out -- MARGE You're late for work, Homer. HOMER So? Someone'll punch in for me. Homer starts to exit. LISA Try not to spill anything, Dad. BART Keep those mutants comin', Homer. INT. POWER PLANT - DAY Scene u

Homer crosses to snack area and looks at the last pickings of the donuts.

REVISED TABLE DRAFT 3/20/90 PAGE 6. Scene 4. (cont+) HOMER (DISAPPOINTED) Oh, man. Plain cake donuts? Thanks for taking all the fancies, guys. (MUMBLING TO HIMSELF) Why can't I ever get here on time? We HEAR a CRACKLING over the PA system. SMITHERS (V.O.) (OVER P.A.) Attention all personnel. Here is an important announcement from our boss, friend and role model, Mr. Burns. Homer and Lenny look up at PA speaker. BURNS (V.0.) (OVER P.A.- FRIENDLY) Hi ho faceless employees. In a few moments the government inspection team will be touring the plant. EXT. SECURITY CLEARANCE BOOTH AT POWER PLANT MR. BURNS is speaking through a microphone that is being held by SMITHERS. Behind them is a banner reading: "WELCOME GOVERNMENT INSPECTION TEAM." BURNS (CONT'D) (THREATENING) So look busy and keep your mouths shut. That is all.

The microphone CLICKS OFF. A white VAN with flags on its fender PULLS UP to the security gate.

SMITHERS

Very stirring, sir.

REVISED TABLE DRAFT 3/20/90 PAGE 7.

Scene 5

A TEAM of white lab-coated inspectors with geiger counters and bulletin boards emerge from the van.

CHIEF INSPECTOR Montgomery Burns?

BURNS My friends call me Red.

CHIEF INSPECTOR Okay, "Red". Shall we begin the inspection?

BURNS Certainly, certainly, barring the possibility we can avoid it somehow.

CHIEF INSPECTOR

(TO TEAM) Okay men, geiger counters on. The geiger counters immediately begin to CRACKLE and POP. BURNS (CHUCKLES WEAKLY) I suppose that's normal background radiation? The kind you would find in any well-maintained nuclear facility? or, for that matter, playgrounds and hospitals. CHIEF INSPECTOR Sorry, Red. We begin a MONTAGE of the inspection. NOTE: The geiger counters will CRACKLE and POP through every scene. The

Chief Inspector dictates each violation to clipboard toting ASSISTANT.

REVISED TABLE DRAFT 3/20/90 PAGE 8. Seene 5 (eon't) EXT. COOLING TOWER - DAY CHIEF INSPECTOR Gum used to seal crack in cooling tower. BURNS I'm as shocked as you are. INT. POWER PLANT - WORK STATION CHIEF INSPECTOR Plutonium rod used as paperweight. BURNS Now, that shouldn't be. INT. POWER PLANT - ROOM WITH A LOT OF PIPES We follow a big pipe which is DRIPPING glowing fluid with ELECTRIC SPARKS coming off it. We follow one of the DRIPS down as it falls onto the clipboard of one of the inspection team, BURNING a hole through it. BURNS (DISMISSING) That's always been like that.

INT. POWER PLANT - OUTSIDE SECONDARY MONITORING STATION

Smithers opens the door. Homer is asleep inside. The arrows in the gauges are all in the red danger zone.

HOMER (WAKENS WITH A SHRIEK) Uh, uh, just resting my eyes.

BURNS Ah, well done. A rested employee is a

vigilant employee.

REVISED TABLE DRAFT 3/20/90 PAGE 9.

Scene 5 (eorst)

CHIEF INSPECTOR Monitoring station unmanned. INT. ANOTHER PART OF POWER PLANT

Burns, Smithers and Inspection Team are standing knee-deep in heavy water.

BURNS (RE CLACKING OF GEIGER COUNTERS) Will you shut those damn things off. They're driving me mad! Everyone TURNS OFF their geiger counters. BURNS Look here, Inspector. Can I speak to you privately in my office? INT. BURNS' OFFICE Scene 6

There is a huge pile of money on the coffee table. Burns, Smithers and the Chief Inspector enter.

CHIEF INSPECTOR Mr. Burns in twenty years I've never seen such a shoddy, deplorable -- BURNS Oh, look! Some careless person has left thousands and thousands of dollars just lying here on my coffee table. Smithers, why don't we leave the room and hopefully, when we return the pile of money will be gone. Burns and Smithers leave the room for a BEAT. They stick

their heads back in the room, register their displeasure, and re-enter.

REVISED TABLE DRAFT 3/20/90 PAGE 10. Scene b (eon't) BURNS (CONT'D) Look, Smithers. The money still hasn't been claimed. Did you notice it appears to be in unmarked tens and twenties? Why don't we step outside again and hopefully when we return, my coffee table will have been tidied up.

They leave again. The Inspector folds his arms resolutely. After a BEAT, Burns and Smithers come back into room.

BURNS (CONT'D) (THROUGH HIS TEETH) Look Smithers, the money and a very stupid man are still here.

CHIEF INSPECTOR Burns, if I didn't know better I'd think you were trying to bribe me.

BURNS Is there some confusion about this?

Burns begins stuffing the money in the Chief Inspector's pockets.

BURNS (CONT'D) (ANGRILY) Take it! Take it! Take it

and spend it on things you like.

REVISED TABLE DRAFT 3/20/90 PAGE 11.

Seene & (eon t)

CHIEF INSPECTOR Mr. Burns, I am going to overlook this felony, however, I will not overlook the three hundred and forty-two violations I have observed at your plant today. Either bring this place up to code or we'll shut it down.

The Inspector hands Burns a list of violations and exits. Burns goes to sit at his desk. He is stunned.

BURNS Tell me, Smithers. What kind of man would want to shut down a nuclear power plant? Oh, well... a little dab of paint here, a little spackle there... how much could it possibly cost to fix this place up?

Smithers is working on an adding machine. He hits the "total" button.

SMITHERS Approximately fifty-six million dollars, sir.

BURNS (ANGRILY) Fifty-six million!

SMITHERS

(FRIGHTENED) Don't hit me, sir.

REVISED TABLE DRAFT 3/20/90 PAGE 12. Scene 6 Ceon't) BURNS (DEFEATED) Oh that I had the strength to take it out on you, Smithers. Now please go. I would be alone. Burns reaches into his desk drawer and pulls out a crystal decanter of 300 year old cognac. He takes a swig. We PAN to the clock on Burns' desk. The room darkens as we,

MATCH DISSOLVE TO:

The clock which reads nine-thirty at night. We PAN back to Burns. The decanter is almost empty. Scene 7

BURNS (CONT'D) (MELANCHOLY SINGING) "Once I built a railroad / made it run / made it race against time/ once I built a railroad / now it's done / brother can you spare a dime."

Burns picks up the decanter of cognac, gets up from his desk and staggers out of the office. 2 Scene

INT. POWER PLANT - OUTSIDE SECONDARY MONITORING STATION

Burns staggers through the dark and deserted hallway, holding his decanter of cognac. He is still singing.

BURNS (CONT'D) (MELANCHOLY SINGING) "When I was thirty-five / it was a very good year / it was a very good year for blue- blooded girls of independent means / we'd ride in limousines.

Burns attempts to take another swig of cognac. He realizes the decanter is empty.

REVISED TABLE DRAFT 3/20/90 PAGE 13. Scene 3 (eon +) BURNS (CONT'D) Empty! Bah!!

He SMASHES the decanter against the door and staggers out of frame.

INT. POWER PLANT - SECONDARY MONITORING STATION - NIGHT Homer awakes with a start. Scene HOMER Ah! (WAKING) Uh... I'm just resting my eyes. (LOOKS AT HIS WATCH) Holy moly! Nine-thirty. Homer picks up the phone and DIAL8. HOMER (CONT'D) (INTO PHONE) Hello, Marge. Sorry I didn't call but it's been a madhouse down here... Yeah, these twelve hour days are killing me. He HANGS UP the phone and exits. INT. POWER PLANT - OUTSIDE SECONDARY MONITORING STATION Homer walks, his footsteps ECHOING, through the dark and deserted power plant. A GLOWING MOUSE scurries across his path. HOMER (CONT'D) Oooh, spooky. EXT. POWER PLANT - PARKING LOT Scene fe) There are only two cars in the parking lot. one is a 1948

Rolls Royce sheltered under a small canopy. The other is Homer's. Homer reacts to the SINGING in the distance.

INT. ROLLS ROYCE - NIGHT Scene. l]

Burns breaks down and begins to 80B. Unseen, Homer enters frame and observes Burns weeping. Homer looks concerned.

HOMER (TAPPING ON CAR WINDOW) Mr. Burns? BURNS (STARTLED) Ah! HOMER Sorry, sir. It's just me, Homer Simpson. Is everything all right? BURNS Working late, Simpson? HOMER Uh... yes, sir. BURNS We're birds of a feather, Simpson. You love this grande dame of a plant as

much as I do, don't you?

(USING BURNS' PRONUNCIATION OF "DAME")

HOMER

Yeah, well, I guess I do love the dame plant...

BURNS I'm going to share something with you, Simpson. Hop in.

With a GRUNT, Burns opens the passenger door and Homer sits beside him.

HOMER (BOUNCING ON SEAT) Ooooh, cushy. BURNS Homer, they're trying to shut us down. They say we're contaminating the planet. HOMER Well nobody's perfect. BURNS You and I are a dying breed, Simpson. Why can't the government just get off our backs? HOMER I was just telling the wife that if I was governor, I'd do things a lot

differently.

REVISED TABLE DRAFT 3/20/90 PAGE 16. ate Seene II (con't) (ANGRILY) Oh, get off your soapbox, Simpson! Do you realize how much it costs to run for office? HOMER More than any honest man could afford. I bet you could afford it. Burns turns and glares at Homer. HOMER Uh... don't get me wrong. I mean you're an honest man. I just meant you could afford to run for governor if you felt like it. "Course I'm just rambling because... uh... because you keep staring at me like that, but it's true. I mean, if you were governor, you could decide what's safe and what isn't.

Burns eyes widen. He turns and starts the IGNITION, pulls out of the parking lot and SCREECHES as drives away.

HOMER Where are we going, sir? BURNS To create a new and better world. HOMER (TIMIDLY) If it's on the way, can you drop me off at my house?

FADE OUT.

Burns and Smithers are sitting at a huge conference table with a DOZEN Madison Avenue-types. There are piles of reports, dozens of charts and graphs scattered around. DEAN DANIELSON Here's the problem as I see it: While Governor Bailey is beloved by all, ninety-eight percent of the voters rate you as despicable or worse. INT. STUDIO Scene 19 Danielson and the others look horrified. BURNS (CONT'D) (INTO CAMERA, REALIZING) Oh, hello friends. I'm Montgomery Burns, your next governor, and I'm here to talk to you about this little guy.

He holds up a goldfish bowl in which a three eyed fish is swimming.

BURNS (CONT'D) Many of you consider him to be a hideous genetic mutation. Nothing could be further from the truth. But don't take my word for it. Let's ask an actor portraying Charles Darwin what he thinks. An actor dressed as CHARLES DARWIN enters. DARWIN Hello, Mr. Burns. BURNS Hello, Charles. Be a good fellow and tell our viewers about your theory of

natural selection.

REVISED TABLE DRAFT 3/20/90 PAGE 25.

Scene !T (con't)

DARWIN Glad to, Mr. Burns. You see, every so often Mother Nature changes her animals giving them bigger teeth, sharper claws, longer legs or, in this case, a third eye. And if these variations turn out to be an improvement, the new animals thrive and multiply and spread across the face of the earth.

BURNS So you're saying this fish might have an advantage over other fish. It may in fact be a kind of super fish!

DARWIN I wouldn't mind having a third eye, would you?

BURNS

(CHUCKLES) No. You see, friends...

Burns gets up and crosses to a live elephant.

Burns

BURNS (CONT'D) «+. if our anti-nuclear naysayers and choose-up-siders were to come upon an elephant frolicking in the waters next to our nuclear power plant they'd probably blame his ridiculous nose on

the nuclear bogeyman.

crosses to a live giraffe.

REVISED TABLE DRAFT 3/20/90 PAGE 26. sees ceeieies Scene |] (con't) Or if they were to chance upon a friendly giraffe with its... (TRYING TO REMEMBER) Uh... uh... boldly patterned hide...

DANIELSON (0.S.) (WHISPERING) Long neck.

BURNS »-.and its long neck, they'd be horrified and want to enforce all sorts of lame-brained safety regulations. The truth is, this fish is a miracle of nature with a taste that can't be beat. So, to summarize: Say what you want about me, I can take the slings and arrows. But stop slandering this poor, defenseless fish. Good night and God

bless.

We DROP DOWN and see the state flag of the state in which Springfield is a city. It's motto: "Not Just Another State." We DROP DOWN to the steps of the Governor's Mansion where MARY BAILEY is addressing a GROUP of reporters. MARY BAILEY

My worthy opponent seems to think that

the voters of this state are gullible

fools. I, however, prefer to rely on

their intelligence and good judgment.

Have you found any dirt on Mary Bailey? INVESTIGATOR #1

Scene 34 (eoi4)

Burns stands and starts knocking bric-a-brac off tables and shelves, staggering around the room. The Simpsons are horrified, but just sit there uncomfortable. Burns staggers up to a side table to knock it over. He GRUNTS FEEBLY but fails to turn it over. BURNS (CONT'D) Smithers, tip this table for me!

Smithers dutifully sends the table CRASHING to the ground. They continue breaking stuff.

MARGE (LOW) Homer... Homer... make them stop.

HOMER (CLEARS THROAT THEN, VERY POLITELY) Uh, Mr. Burns... Mr. Burns...

BURNS Shut up and wreck something.

Homer dutifully picks up a vase and SMASHES it. Marge has her arm around Lisa.

LISA Mr. Burns, I hardly see what destroying our meager possessions is going to accomplish.

BURNS (SIGHS) She's right. Take me hone, Smithers. We'll destroy something tasteful.

Burns and Smithers start out front door, then stop.

REVISED TABLE DRAFT 3/20/90 PAGE 47. Scene 34 (ert)

BURNS (CONT'D) (TO SMITHERS) Ironic, isn't it Smithers? This anonymous clan of slack-jawed troglodytes has cost me the election, and yet if I were to have them killed, I would be the one to go to jail. That's democracy for you.

SMITHERS You are noble and poetic in defeat, sir.

BURNS: (TO HOMER) Simpson, I shall make it the focus of my remaining years that your dreams will go unfulfilled.

Burns and Smithers exit.

HOMER What?

BART Uh oh. You're busted, Dad.

HOMER My dreams shall remain unfulfilled! I don't like the sound of that one bit. That means I have nothing to hope for. Marge, make it better. Please. Can't

you make it better, huh?

MARGE When a man's dreams are seconds on dessert, occasional snuggling and sleeping till eleven o'clock on weekends, no man can destroy them.
